From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Style of painting and sculpture

"Academist" redirects here; not to be confused with [Academician](/wiki/Academician "Academician") or [Academic](/wiki/Academic_(disambiguation) "Academic (disambiguation)").

**Academic art**, **academicism**, or **academism**, is a style of [painting](/wiki/Painting "Painting") and [sculpture](/wiki/Sculpture "Sculpture") produced under the influence of European [academies of art](/wiki/Academies_of_art "Academies of art"). This method extended its influence throughout the [Western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") over several centuries, from its origins in Italy in the mid-16th century, until its dissipation in the early 20th century. It reached its apogee in the 19th century, after the end of the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars") in 1815. In this period, the standards of the French [Académie des Beaux-Arts](/wiki/Acad%C3%A9mie_des_Beaux-Arts "Académie des Beaux-Arts") were very influential, combining elements of [Neoclassicism](/wiki/Neoclassicism "Neoclassicism") and [Romanticism](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism"), with [Jean-Auguste-Dominique Ingres](/wiki/Jean-Auguste-Dominique_Ingres "Jean-Auguste-Dominique Ingres") a key figure in the formation of the style in painting. The success of the French model led to the founding of countless other art academies in several countries. Later painters who tried to continue the synthesis included [William-Adolphe Bouguereau](/wiki/William-Adolphe_Bouguereau "William-Adolphe Bouguereau"), [Thomas Couture](/wiki/Thomas_Couture "Thomas Couture"), and [Hans Makart](/wiki/Hans_Makart "Hans Makart") among many others. In sculpture, academic art is characterized by a tendency towards monumentality, as in the works of [Auguste Bartholdi](/wiki/Auguste_Bartholdi "Auguste Bartholdi") and [Daniel Chester French](/wiki/Daniel_Chester_French "Daniel Chester French").

The academies were established to replace medieval artists' [guilds](/wiki/Guild "Guild") and aimed to systematize the teaching of art. They emphasized the emulation of established masters and the classical tradition, downplaying the importance of individual creativity, valuing instead collective, [aesthetic](/wiki/Aesthetics "Aesthetics") and [ethical](/wiki/Ethics "Ethics") concepts. By helping raise the professional status of artists, the academies distanced them from artisans and brought them closer to intellectuals. They also played a crucial role in organizing the [art world](/wiki/Art_world "Art world"), controlling cultural [ideology](/wiki/Ideology "Ideology"), taste, criticism, the [art market](/wiki/Art_market "Art market"), as well as the exhibition and dissemination of art. They wielded significant influence due to their association with state power, often acting as conduits for the dissemination of artistic, political, and social ideals, by deciding what was considered "official art". As a result, they faced criticism and controversy from artists and others on the margins of these academic circles, and their restrictive and [universalist](/wiki/Universalism "Universalism") regulations are sometimes considered a reflection of [absolutism](/wiki/Absolutism_(European_history) "Absolutism (European history)").

Overall, academicism has had a significant impact on the development of art education and artistic styles. Its artists rarely showed interest in depicting the everyday or profane. Thus, academic art is predominantly [idealistic](/wiki/Idealism_(arts) "Idealism (arts)") rather than [realistic](/wiki/Realism_(arts) "Realism (arts)"), aiming to create highly polished works through the mastery of color and form. Although smaller works such as [portraits](/wiki/Portrait "Portrait"), [landscapes](/wiki/Landscape_painting "Landscape painting") and [still-lifes](/wiki/Still-life "Still-life") were also produced, the movement and the contemporary public and critics most valued large [history paintings](/wiki/History_painting "History painting") showing moments from narratives that were very often taken from ancient or exotic areas of history and [mythology](/wiki/Mythology "Mythology"), though less often the traditional [religious narratives](/wiki/Religious_art "Religious art"). [Orientalist art](/wiki/Orientalism#Orientalist_art "Orientalism") was a major branch, with [many specialist painters](/wiki/List_of_Orientalist_artists "List of Orientalist artists"), as were scenes from [classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity") and the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"). Academic art is also closely related to [Beaux-Arts architecture](/wiki/Beaux-Arts_architecture "Beaux-Arts architecture"), as well as [classical music](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music") and [dance](/wiki/Ballet "Ballet"), which developed simultaneously and hold to a similar classicizing ideal.

Although production of academic art continued into the 20th century, the style had become vacuous, and was strongly rejected by the artists of set of new [art movements](/wiki/Art_movement "Art movement"), of which [Realism](/wiki/Realism_(arts) "Realism (arts)") and [Impressionism](/wiki/Impressionism "Impressionism") were some of the first. In this context, the style is often called "[eclecticism](/wiki/Eclecticism_in_art "Eclecticism in art")", "[art pompier](/wiki/L%27art_pompier "L'art pompier")" (pejoratively), and sometimes linked with "[historicism](/wiki/Historicism_(art) "Historicism (art)")" and "[syncretism](/wiki/Syncretism "Syncretism")". By [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), it had fallen from favor almost completely with critics and buyers, before regaining some appreciation since the end of the 20th century.

## Origins and theoretical foundations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Academic_art&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Origins and theoretical foundations")]

### The first art academies in Renaissance Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Academic_art&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: The first art academies in Renaissance Italy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Zucchi%2C_Jacopo_-_Vasari%2C_Giorgio_-_Uffizi_ICCD.jpg/250px-Zucchi%2C_Jacopo_-_Vasari%2C_Giorgio_-_Uffizi_ICCD.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zucchi,_Jacopo_-_Vasari,_Giorgio_-_Uffizi_ICCD.jpg)

[Giorgio Vasari](/wiki/Giorgio_Vasari "Giorgio Vasari") helped found the [Accademia e Compagnia delle Arti del Disegno](/wiki/Accademia_delle_Arti_del_Disegno "Accademia delle Arti del Disegno") (Academy and Company for the Arts of Drawing) in 1563

The first academy of art was founded in [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence") by [Cosimo I de' Medici](/wiki/Cosimo_I_de%27_Medici "Cosimo I de' Medici"), on 13 January 1563, under the influence of the architect [Giorgio Vasari](/wiki/Giorgio_Vasari "Giorgio Vasari"), who called it the [Accademia e Compagnia delle Arti del Disegno](/wiki/Accademia_delle_Arti_del_Disegno "Accademia delle Arti del Disegno") (Academy and Company for the Arts of Drawing) as it was divided in two different operative branches. While the company was a kind of corporation that every working artist in Tuscany could join, the academy comprised only the most eminent artists of Cosimo's court, and had the task of overseeing all Florentine artistic activities, including teaching, and safeguarding local cultural traditions. Among the founding members were [Michelangelo](/wiki/Michelangelo "Michelangelo"), [Bartolomeo Ammannati](/wiki/Bartolomeo_Ammannati "Bartolomeo Ammannati"), [Agnolo Bronzino](/wiki/Agnolo_Bronzino "Agnolo Bronzino") and [Francesco da Sangallo](/wiki/Francesco_da_Sangallo "Francesco da Sangallo"). In this institution, students learned the "arti del disegno" (a term coined by Vasari) and heard lectures on [anatomy](/wiki/Anatomy "Anatomy") and [geometry](/wiki/Geometry "Geometry").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) The Accademia's fame spread quickly, to the point that, within just five months of its founding, important [Venetian](/wiki/Venice "Venice") artists such as [Titian](/wiki/Titian "Titian"), [Salviati](/wiki/Francesco_Salviati_(painter) "Francesco Salviati (painter)"), [Tintoretto](/wiki/Tintoretto "Tintoretto") and [Palladio](/wiki/Palladio "Palladio") applied for admission, and in 1567, King [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain") consulted it about plans for [El Escorial](/wiki/El_Escorial "El Escorial").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Another academy, the Accademia de i Pittori e Scultori di Roma (Academy of Painters and Sculptors of Rome), better known as the [Accademia di San Luca](/wiki/Accademia_di_San_Luca "Accademia di San Luca") (named after the patron saint of painters, [St. Luke](/wiki/St._Luke "St. Luke")), was founded about a decade later in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"). It served an educational function and was more concerned with [art theory](/wiki/Art_theory "Art theory") than the Florentine one, attaching great importance to attending theoretical lectures, debates and drawing classes.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Twelve academics were immediately appointed as teachers, establishing a series of disciplinary measures for studies and instituting a system of awards for the most capable students.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In 1582, the painter and art instructor [Annibale Carracci](/wiki/Annibale_Carracci "Annibale Carracci") opened his very influential [Accademia dei Desiderosi](/wiki/Accademia_degli_Incamminati "Accademia degli Incamminati") (Academy of the Desirous) in [Bologna](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna") without official support; in some ways, this was more like a traditional artist's studio, but that he felt the need to label it as an "academy" demonstrates the attraction of the idea at the time.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Academic_students.jpg/250px-Academic_students.jpg)](/wiki/File:Academic_students.jpg)

[Carlo Maratti](/wiki/Carlo_Maratti "Carlo Maratti"), *The Academy of Drawing*, c. 1704–1709. An allegory of academic teaching dedicated "to young students of drawing".

The emergence of art academies in the 16th century was due to the need to respond to new social demands. Several states, which were moving towards [absolutism](/wiki/Absolutism_(European_history) "Absolutism (European history)"), realized that it was necessary to create an art that specifically identified them and served as a symbol of civic unity, and was also capable of symbolically consolidating the status of their rulers. In this process, the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), then the greatest political force and social unifier in Europe, began to lose some of its influence as a result of the greater [secularization](/wiki/Secularization "Secularization") of societies. [Sacred art](/wiki/Sacred_art "Sacred art"), by far the largest field of artistic expression throughout the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), came to coexist with an expanding profane art, derived from [classical](/wiki/Classical_Antiquity "Classical Antiquity") sources, which had been experiencing a slow revival since the 12th century, and which, by the time of the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"), had been established as the most prestigious cultural reference and model of quality.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

This re-emergence of [classicism](/wiki/Classicism "Classicism") required artists to become more cultured, in order to competently transpose this reference to the [visual arts](/wiki/Visual_arts "Visual arts"). At the same time, the old system of artistic production, organized by [guilds](/wiki/Guild "Guild")—class associations of an artisanal nature, linked more to mechanical crafts than to intellectual erudition—began to be seen as outdated and socially unworthy, as artists began to desire equality with the intellectual versed in the [liberal arts](/wiki/Liberal_art "Liberal art"), since art itself began to be seen not only as a technical task, as it had been for centuries, but mainly as a way of acquiring and transmitting knowledge. In this new context, painting and sculpture began to be seen as theorizable, just as other arts such as literature and especially poetry were already. However, if on the one hand the artists did rise socially, on the other they lost the security of market insertion that the guild system provided, having to live in the uncertain expectation of individual protection by some [patron](/wiki/Patronage "Patronage").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Standardization: French academicism and visual arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Academic_art&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Standardization: French academicism and visual arts")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Charles_Le_Brun_001.jpg/250px-Charles_Le_Brun_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_Le_Brun_001.jpg)

[Charles Le Brun](/wiki/Charles_Le_Brun "Charles Le Brun"), *Apotheosis of Louis XIV*, 1677. An example of art at the service of the State.

If Italy was to be credited with founding this new type of institution, France was responsible for taking the model to a first stage of great order and stability. The country's first attempts to establish academies like the Italian ones also took place in the 16th century, during the reign of King [Henry III](/wiki/Henry_III_of_France "Henry III of France"), especially through the work of the poet [Jean-Antoine de Baïf](/wiki/Jean-Antoine_de_Ba%C3%AFf "Jean-Antoine de Baïf"), who founded an academy linked to the [French Crown](/wiki/List_of_French_monarchs "List of French monarchs"). Like its Italian counterparts, it was primarily [philological](/wiki/Philology "Philology")-[philosophical](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy") in nature, but it also worked on concepts relating to the arts and sciences. Although it developed intense activity with regular debates and theoretical production, defending classical principles, it lacked an educational structure and had a brief existence.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The Accademia di San Luca later served as the model for the French [Académie royale de peinture et de sculpture](/wiki/Acad%C3%A9mie_royale_de_peinture_et_de_sculpture "Académie royale de peinture et de sculpture") (Royal Academy of Painting and Sculpture), founded in 1648 by a group of artists led by [Charles Le Brun](/wiki/Charles_Le_Brun "Charles Le Brun"), and which later became the [Académie des Beaux-Arts](/wiki/Acad%C3%A9mie_des_Beaux-Arts "Académie des Beaux-Arts") (Academy of Fine Arts). Its objective was similar to the Italian one, to honor artists "who were gentlemen practicing a liberal art" from craftsmen, who were engaged in manual labor. This emphasis on the intellectual component of artmaking had a considerable impact on the subjects and styles of academic art.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETestelin185322–36-12)[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMontaiglonCornu18757–10-13)[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDussieuxSouliéMantzMontaiglon1854216-14)

After an ineffective start, the Académie royale was reorganized in 1661 by King [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV"), whose aim was to control all the country's artistic activity,[[15]](#cite_note-15) and in 1671, it came under the control of [First Minister of State](/wiki/Chief_minister_of_France "Chief minister of France") [Jean-Baptiste Colbert](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Colbert "Jean-Baptiste Colbert"), who confirmed Le Brun as director. Together, they made it the main executive arm of a program to glorify the king's [absolutist monarchy](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy"), definitively establishing the school's association with the State and thereby vesting it with enormous directive power over the entire national art system, which contributed to making France the new European cultural center, displacing the hitherto Italian supremacy. But while for the Italian Renaissance, art was also a survey of the natural world, for Le Brun it was above all the product of an acquired culture, inherited forms and an established tradition.[[16]](#cite_note-Barasch-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

During this period, academic doctrine reached the peak of its rigor, comprehensiveness, uniformity, formalism and explicitness, and according to art historian Moshe Barasch, at no other time in the history of [art theory](/wiki/Theory_of_art "Theory of art") has the idea of perfection been more intensely cultivated as the artist's highest goal, with the production of the Italian [High Renaissance](/wiki/High_Renaissance "High Renaissance") as the ultimate model. Thus, Italy continued to be an invaluable reference, so much so that a branch was established in Rome in 1666, the [French Academy](/wiki/French_Academy_in_Rome "French Academy in Rome"), with [Charles Errard](/wiki/Charles_Errard "Charles Errard") as its first director.[[16]](#cite_note-Barasch-16)

At the same time, a controversy occurred among the members of the Académie, which would come to dominate artistic attitudes for the rest of the century. This "battle of styles" was a conflict over whether [Peter Paul Rubens](/wiki/Peter_Paul_Rubens "Peter Paul Rubens") or [Nicolas Poussin](/wiki/Nicolas_Poussin "Nicolas Poussin") was a suitable model to follow. Followers of Poussin, called "poussinistes", argued that line (disegno) should dominate art, because of its appeal to the intellect, while followers of Rubens, called "rubenistes", argued that color (colore) should be the dominant feature, because of its appeal to emotion.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The debate was revived in the early 19th century, under the movements of [Neoclassicism](/wiki/Neoclassicism "Neoclassicism") typified by the art of [Jean Auguste Dominique Ingres](/wiki/Jean_Auguste_Dominique_Ingres "Jean Auguste Dominique Ingres"), and [Romanticism](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism") typified by the artwork of [Eugène Delacroix](/wiki/Eug%C3%A8ne_Delacroix "Eugène Delacroix"). Debates also occurred over whether it was better to learn art by looking at nature, or at the artistic masters of the past.[[19]](#cite_note-Tanner,_Jeremy_2003-19)

### Transformations and diffusion of the French model

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Academic_art&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Transformations and diffusion of the French model")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Wilhelm_Bendz_-_The_Life_Class_at_the_Royal_Academy_of_Fine_Arts%2C_1826.jpg/330px-Wilhelm_Bendz_-_The_Life_Class_at_the_Royal_Academy_of_Fine_Arts%2C_1826.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wilhelm_Bendz_-_The_Life_Class_at_the_Royal_Academy_of_Fine_Arts,_1826.jpg)

[Wilhelm Bendz](/wiki/Wilhelm_Bendz "Wilhelm Bendz"), *The Life Class at the [Royal Academy of Fine Arts](/wiki/Royal_Danish_Academy_of_Fine_Arts "Royal Danish Academy of Fine Arts")* in [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen "Copenhagen"), 1826

At the end of Louis XIV's reign, the academic style and teachings strongly associated with his monarchy began to spread throughout Europe, accompanying the growth of the urban nobility. A series of other important academies were formed across the continent, inspired by the success of the French Académie: the [Akademie der Bildenden Künste](/wiki/Academy_of_Fine_Arts,_Nuremberg "Academy of Fine Arts, Nuremberg") in [Nuremberg](/wiki/Nuremberg "Nuremberg") (1662), the [Royal Academy of Fine Arts](/wiki/Royal_Academy_of_Fine_Arts_(Antwerp) "Royal Academy of Fine Arts (Antwerp)") in [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") (1663), the [Akademie der Künste](/wiki/Academy_of_Arts,_Berlin "Academy of Arts, Berlin") in [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") (1696), the [Akademie der bildenden Künste](/wiki/Academy_of_Fine_Arts_Vienna "Academy of Fine Arts Vienna") in [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna") (1698), the [Royal Drawing Academy](/wiki/Royal_Swedish_Academy_of_Fine_Arts "Royal Swedish Academy of Fine Arts") in [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm") (1735), the [Real Academia de Bellas Artes de San Fernando](/wiki/Real_Academia_de_Bellas_Artes_de_San_Fernando "Real Academia de Bellas Artes de San Fernando") in [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid") (1752), the [Imperial Academy of Arts](/wiki/Imperial_Academy_of_Arts "Imperial Academy of Arts") in [Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg "Saint Petersburg") (1757), and the [Accademia di Belle Arti di Brera](/wiki/Accademia_di_Belle_Arti_di_Brera "Accademia di Belle Arti di Brera") in [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") (1776), to name a few. In England, this was the [Royal Academy of Arts](/wiki/Royal_Academy_of_Arts "Royal Academy of Arts"), which was founded in 1768 with a mission "to establish a school or academy of design for the use of students in the arts".[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHodgsonEaton190511-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The [Royal Danish Academy of Fine Arts](/wiki/Royal_Danish_Academy_of_Fine_Arts "Royal Danish Academy of Fine Arts") in [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen "Copenhagen"), founded in 1754, may be taken as a successful example in a smaller country, which achieved its aim of producing a national school and reducing the reliance on imported artists. The painters of the [Danish Golden Age](/wiki/Danish_Golden_Age "Danish Golden Age") of roughly 1800–1850 were nearly all trained there, and drawing on Italian and [Dutch Golden Age paintings](/wiki/Dutch_Golden_Age_painting "Dutch Golden Age painting") as examples, many returned to teach locally.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The history of [Danish art](/wiki/Danish_art "Danish art") is much less marked by tension between academic art and other styles than is the case in other countries.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the 18th and 19th centuries, the model expanded to America, with the [Academy of San Carlos](/wiki/Academy_of_San_Carlos "Academy of San Carlos") in Mexico being founded in 1783, the [Pennsylvania Academy of Fine Arts](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Academy_of_Fine_Arts "Pennsylvania Academy of Fine Arts") in the United States in 1805,[[23]](#cite_note-kemp-23) and the [Imperial Academy of Fine Arts](/wiki/Imperial_Academy_of_Fine_Arts_(Brazil) "Imperial Academy of Fine Arts (Brazil)") in Brazil in 1826.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Meanwhile, back in Italy, another major center of irradiation appeared, Venice, launching the tradition of urban views and "[capriccios](/wiki/Capriccio_(art) "Capriccio (art)")", fantasy landscape scenes populated by ancient ruins, which became favorites of noble travelers on the [Grand Tour](/wiki/Grand_Tour "Grand Tour").[[23]](#cite_note-kemp-23)

## Development of the academic style

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Academic_art&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Development of the academic style")]

### Early challenges: the Enlightenment and Romanticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Academic_art&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Early challenges: the Enlightenment and Romanticism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Denis_Diderot_by_Louis-Michel_van_Loo.jpg/250px-Denis_Diderot_by_Louis-Michel_van_Loo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Denis_Diderot_by_Louis-Michel_van_Loo.jpg)

Despite being challenged by most, some [Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") figures like [Diderot](/wiki/Diderot "Diderot") ([portrayed](/wiki/Portrait_of_Denis_Diderot "Portrait of Denis Diderot"), 1767) subscribed to much of the academic ideal.

Even with its wide spread, the academic system began to be seriously challenged through the actions of intellectuals linked to the [Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment"). For them, academicism had become an outdated model, excessively rigid and dogmatic; they criticized the methodology, which they believed produced an art that was merely servile to ancient examples, and condemned the institutional administration, which they considered corrupt and [despotic](/wiki/Despotism "Despotism").[[25]](#cite_note-Schwarcz-25) However, an important Enlightenment figure like [Diderot](/wiki/Diderot "Diderot") subscribed to much of the academic ideal, supported the [hierarchy of genres](/wiki/Hierarchy_of_genres "Hierarchy of genres") (*see below*), and said that "the imagination creates nothing".[[26]](#cite_note-26) At the end of the 18th century, following the turmoil of the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), a real campaign was mounted against the teaching of the academy, which was identified as a symbol of the [Ancien Régime](/wiki/Ancien_R%C3%A9gime "Ancien Régime"). In 1793, the painter [Jacques-Louis David](/wiki/Jacques-Louis_David "Jacques-Louis David"), closely linked to the revolutionaries, took over the direction of the artistic affairs of the [new republic](/wiki/French_First_Republic "French First Republic") and, after complying with the request of numerous artists dissatisfied with the institution's bureaucracy and system of privileges, dissolved the Parisian academies and all the other royal academies in the countryside. However, the extinction of the old schools was temporary, as a Committee for the Arts was subsequently organized, which led to the founding in 1795 of a new institution, the [Institut de France](/wiki/Institut_de_France "Institut de France"), which included an artistic section and was responsible for reorganizing the national arts system.[[25]](#cite_note-Schwarcz-25)

The challenges to academicism in France, however, were more nominal than real. Art courses returned to operating in broadly the same way as before, the hierarchy of genres was resurrected, the awards and salons were maintained, the branch in Rome remained active, and the State continued to be the biggest sponsor of art. [Quatremère de Quincy](/wiki/Quatrem%C3%A8re_de_Quincy "Quatremère de Quincy"), the secretary of the new Institut, which had been born as an apparatus of revolutionary renewal, paradoxically believed that art schools served to preserve traditions, not to found new ones.[[25]](#cite_note-Schwarcz-25) The greatest innovations he introduced were the idea of reunifying the arts under an atmosphere of egalitarianism, eliminating honorary titles for members and some other privileges, and his attempt to make administration more transparent, eminently public and functional. In reinterpreting the [Platonic](/wiki/Plato "Plato") theory that the arts are questionable because they are imperfect imitations of an abstract [ideal reality](/wiki/Idealism_(art) "Idealism (art)"), he considered this idea only in a moral sphere, politicized and republicanized it, relating the truth of the arts to that of social institutions. He also claimed that the political reality of the republic was a reflection of the republic of the arts that he sought to establish. But beyond the ideas, in practice, [authoritarianism](/wiki/Authoritarianism "Authoritarianism"), which was one of the reasons given for the extinction of the royal academies, continued to be practiced in the republican administration.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Thomas_Jones_The_Bard_1774.jpg/330px-Thomas_Jones_The_Bard_1774.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_Jones_The_Bard_1774.jpg)

[Thomas Jones](/wiki/Thomas_Jones_(artist) "Thomas Jones (artist)"), *[The Bard](/wiki/The_Bard_(Jones) "The Bard (Jones)")*, 1774. A prophetic combination of [Romanticism](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism") and [nationalism](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism").

Another attack on the academic model came from the early [Romantics](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism"), at the turn of 18th to 19th centuries, who preached a practice centered on individual originality and independence. Around 1816, the painter [Théodore Géricault](/wiki/Th%C3%A9odore_G%C3%A9ricault "Théodore Géricault"), one of the exponents of French Romanticism, stated:

> These schools keep their pupils in a state of constant emulation... I note with sadness that, since the establishment of these schools, there has been a great effect: they have given service to thousands of mediocre talents... Painters enter there too young, and therefore the traces of individuality that survive the Academy are imperceptible. One can see, with real chagrin, about ten or twelve compositions every year that are practically identical in execution, because in their quest for perfection, they lose their originality. One way of drawing, one type of color, one arrangement for all systems...[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Stylistic trends and contradictions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Academic_art&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Stylistic trends and contradictions")]

Since the onset of the Poussiniste-Rubeniste debate, many artists worked between the two styles. In the 19th century, in the revived form of the debate, the attention and the aims of the art world became to synthesize the line of [Neoclassicism](/wiki/Neoclassicism "Neoclassicism") with the color of Romanticism. One artist after another was claimed by critics to have achieved the synthesis, among them [Théodore Chassériau](/wiki/Th%C3%A9odore_Chass%C3%A9riau "Théodore Chassériau"), [Ary Scheffer](/wiki/Ary_Scheffer "Ary Scheffer"), [Francesco Hayez](/wiki/Francesco_Hayez "Francesco Hayez"), [Alexandre-Gabriel Decamps](/wiki/Alexandre-Gabriel_Decamps "Alexandre-Gabriel Decamps"), and [Thomas Couture](/wiki/Thomas_Couture "Thomas Couture"). [William-Adolphe Bouguereau](/wiki/William-Adolphe_Bouguereau "William-Adolphe Bouguereau"), a later academic artist, commented that the trick to being a good painter is seeing "color and line as the same thing". Thomas Couture promoted the same idea in a book he authored on art method—arguing that whenever one said a painting had better color or better line it was nonsense, because whenever color appeared brilliant it depended on line to convey it, and vice versa; and that color was really a way to talk about the "value" of form.

#### Historicism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Academic_art&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Historicism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Jacques-Louis_David%2C_Le_Serment_des_Horaces.jpg/330px-Jacques-Louis_David%2C_Le_Serment_des_Horaces.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jacques-Louis_David,_Le_Serment_des_Horaces.jpg)

[Jacques-Louis David](/wiki/Jacques-Louis_David "Jacques-Louis David"), *[Oath of the Horatii](/wiki/Oath_of_the_Horatii "Oath of the Horatii")*, 1784. A typical historical work of the [neoclassical](/wiki/Neoclassicism "Neoclassicism") period, politically and ethically engaged.

Another development during this period, called [historicism](/wiki/Historicism_(art) "Historicism (art)"), included adopting historic styles or imitating the work of historic artists and artisans in order to show the era in history that the painting depicted. In the [history of art](/wiki/History_of_art "History of art"), after Neoclassicism which in the Romantic era could itself be considered a historicist movement, the 19th century included a new historicist phase characterized by an interpretation not only of [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") and [Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") [classicism](/wiki/Classicism "Classicism"), but also of succeeding stylistic eras, which were increasingly respected. This is best seen in the work of Baron [Jan August Hendrik Leys](/wiki/Jan_August_Hendrik_Leys "Jan August Hendrik Leys"), a later influence on [James Tissot](/wiki/James_Tissot "James Tissot"). It is also seen in the development of the [Neo-Grec](/wiki/Neo-Grec "Neo-Grec") style. Historicism is also meant to refer to the belief and practice associated with academic art that one should incorporate and conciliate the innovations of different traditions of art from the past.

#### Allegory in art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Academic_art&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Allegory in art")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Thomas_Cole_-_Architect%E2%80%99s_Dream_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/330px-Thomas_Cole_-_Architect%E2%80%99s_Dream_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_Cole_-_Architect%E2%80%99s_Dream_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

[Thomas Cole](/wiki/Thomas_Cole "Thomas Cole"), *[The Architect's Dream](/wiki/The_Architect%27s_Dream "The Architect's Dream")*, 1840. An [allegorical](/wiki/Allegory "Allegory") illustration of [Historicism](/wiki/Historicism "Historicism") inspiring art.

The art world also grew to give increasing focus on [allegory](/wiki/Allegory "Allegory") in art. Theories of the importance of both line and color asserted that through these elements an artist exerts control over the medium to create psychological effects, in which themes, emotions, and ideas can be represented. As artists attempted to synthesize these theories in practice, the attention on the artwork as an allegorical or figurative vehicle was emphasized. It was held that the representations in painting and sculpture should evoke [Platonic forms](/wiki/Platonic_form "Platonic form"), or ideals, where behind ordinary depictions one would glimpse something abstract, some eternal truth. Hence, [Keats'](/wiki/John_Keats "John Keats") famous musing "Beauty is truth, truth beauty". The paintings were desired to be an "idée", a full and complete idea. [Bouguereau](/wiki/Adolphe_William_Bouguereau "Adolphe William Bouguereau") is known to have said that he would not paint "a war", but would paint "War". Many paintings by academic artists are simple nature allegories with titles like *Dawn*, *Dusk*, *Seeing*, and *Tasting*, where these ideas are personified by a single nude figure, composed in such a way as to bring out the essence of the idea.

#### Idealism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Academic_art&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Idealism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Alexandre_Cabanel_-_The_Birth_of_Venus_-_Google_Art_Project_2.jpg/330px-Alexandre_Cabanel_-_The_Birth_of_Venus_-_Google_Art_Project_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alexandre_Cabanel_-_The_Birth_of_Venus_-_Google_Art_Project_2.jpg)

*[The Birth of Venus](/wiki/The_Birth_of_Venus_(Cabanel) "The Birth of Venus (Cabanel)")*, [Alexandre Cabanel](/wiki/Alexandre_Cabanel "Alexandre Cabanel"), 1863. An idealistic and sensual form of classical imagery.

Stylistically, academic art cultivated the ideal of perfection and at the same time selective imitation of reality ([mimesis](/wiki/Mimesis "Mimesis")), which had existed since [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle"). With perfect mastery of color, light and shadow, forms were created in a quasi-photorealistic manner. Some paintings have a "polished finish" where no brushstroke can be recognized on the finished work. After the oil sketch, the artist would produce the final painting with the academic "fini", changing the painting to meet stylistic standards and attempting to idealize the images and add perfect detail. Similarly, [perspective](/wiki/Perspective_(visual) "Perspective (visual)") was constructed geometrically on a flat surface and was not really the product of sight.

The trend in art was also towards greater [idealism](/wiki/Idealism_(arts) "Idealism (arts)"), which is contrary to [realism](/wiki/Realism_(arts) "Realism (arts)"), in that the figures depicted were made simpler and more abstract—idealized—in order to be able to represent the ideals they stood in for. This would involve both generalizing forms seen in nature, and subordinating them to the unity and theme of the artwork.

#### Hierarchy of genres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Academic_art&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Hierarchy of genres")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Makart_hans_der_einzug_karls_v_in_antwerpen.jpg/330px-Makart_hans_der_einzug_karls_v_in_antwerpen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Makart_hans_der_einzug_karls_v_in_antwerpen.jpg)

[Hans Makart](/wiki/Hans_Makart "Hans Makart"), *The Entry of Charles V into Antwerp*, 1878. An illustrative work of the prestigious "historical genre".

The representation of the various emotions was codified in detail by academicism[[29]](#cite_note-29) and the artistic genres themselves were subjected to a scale of prestige. Because history and mythology were considered plays or [dialectics](/wiki/Dialectic "Dialectic") of ideas, a fertile ground for important allegory, using themes from these subjects was regarded as the most serious form of painting. This hierarchy of genres, originally created in the 17th century, was highly valued, where [history painting](/wiki/History_painting "History painting") (also known as the "grande genre")—classical, religious, mythological, literary, and allegorical subjects—was placed at the top, followed by "minor genres"—[portraiture](/wiki/Portrait "Portrait"), [genre painting](/wiki/Genre_works "Genre works"), [landscapes](/wiki/Landscape_painting "Landscape painting"), and [still-lifes](/wiki/Still-life "Still-life").[[30]](#cite_note-Hierarchy-30)[[23]](#cite_note-kemp-23)

The historical genre, the most appreciated, included works that conveyed themes of an inspirational and ennobling nature, essentially with an ethical background, consistent with the tradition founded by masters such as Michelangelo, [Raphael](/wiki/Raffaello_Santi "Raffaello Santi") and [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci"). [Paul Delaroche](/wiki/Paul_Delaroche "Paul Delaroche") is a typifying example of French history painting and [Benjamin West](/wiki/Benjamin_West "Benjamin West") of the [British-American](/wiki/British_America "British America") vogue for painting scenes from recent history. Paintings by [Hans Makart](/wiki/Hans_Makart "Hans Makart") are often larger than life historical dramas, and he combined this with a [historicism](/wiki/Historicism_(art) "Historicism (art)") in decoration to dominate the style of 19th-century [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna") culture. Portraits included large-format depictions of people, suitable for their public glorification, but also smaller pieces for private use. Everyday scenes, also known as genre scenes, portrayed common life in a symbolic manner, landscapes offered perspectives of idealized virgin nature or city panoramas, and still lifes consisted of groupings of diverse objects in formal compositions.[[30]](#cite_note-Hierarchy-30)[[23]](#cite_note-kemp-23)

The justification for this hierarchization lay in the idea that each genre had an inherent and specific moral force. Thus, an artist could convey a moral principle with much more power and ease through a historical scene than, for example, through a still-life. Furthermore, following Greek concepts, it was believed that the highest form of art was the ideal representation of the human body, hence landscapes and still lifes, in which man did not appear, had little prestige. Finally, with a primarily social and didactic function, academic art favored large works and large-format portraits, more suitable for viewing by large groups of spectators and better suited to decorating public spaces.[[30]](#cite_note-Hierarchy-30)[[23]](#cite_note-kemp-23)

All of these trends were influenced by the theories of the [German philosopher](/wiki/German_philosophy "German philosophy") [Hegel](/wiki/Georg_Wilhelm_Friedrich_Hegel "Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel"), who held that history was a [dialectic](/wiki/Dialectic "Dialectic") of competing ideas, which eventually resolved in synthesis.

### Maturation: an increasingly bourgeois art form

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Academic_art&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Maturation: an increasingly bourgeois art form")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Lille_PdBA_lemaire_napoleon.JPG/250px-Lille_PdBA_lemaire_napoleon.JPG)](/wiki/File:Lille_PdBA_lemaire_napoleon.JPG)

[Philippe Joseph Henri Lemaire](/wiki/Philippe_Joseph_Henri_Lemaire "Philippe Joseph Henri Lemaire"), *Napoleon, protector of industry*, 1854. A symbol of glorified "civilizing imperialism".

[Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") was the "[swan song](/wiki/Swan_song "Swan song")" of the concept of art as a vehicle of moral values and a mirror of [virtue](/wiki/Virtue "Virtue"). He actively patronized and employed artists to portray his personal glory, that of his Empire and of his political and military conquests. After him, the fragmentation and weakening of ideals began to become visible and irreversible.[[31]](#cite_note-31) With the cooling of the [libertarian](/wiki/Libertarianism "Libertarianism") ardor of the first Romantics, with the final failure of Napoleon's imperialist project, and with the popularization of an eclectic style that blended Romanticism and Neoclassicism, adapting them to the purposes of the [bourgeoisie](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie"), which became one of the greatest sponsors of the arts, the emergence of a general feeling of resignation appeared, as well as a growing prevalence of individual bourgeois taste against idealistic collective systems. Soon the preferences of this social class, now so influential, penetrated higher education and became worthy objects of representation, changing the hierarchy of genres and proliferating portraits and all the so-called minor genres, such as everyday scenes and still lifes, which became more pronounced as the century progressed.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/%22Le_Crabe%22_by_William-Adolphe_Bouguereau.jpg/250px-%22Le_Crabe%22_by_William-Adolphe_Bouguereau.jpg)](/wiki/File:%22Le_Crabe%22_by_William-Adolphe_Bouguereau.jpg)

[William-Adolphe Bouguereau](/wiki/William-Adolphe_Bouguereau "William-Adolphe Bouguereau"), *The Crab*, 1869. Scenes from common life as a subject worthy of academic representation.

The bourgeoisie's support for academies was a way of demonstrating education and acquiring social prestige, bringing them closer to the cultural and political elites. Finally, [Neo-Gothic](/wiki/Neo-Gothic "Neo-Gothic") revivalism, the development of a taste for the [picturesque](/wiki/Picturesque "Picturesque") as an autonomous aesthetic criterion, the revival of [Hellenistic](/wiki/Greek_art "Greek art") [eclecticism](/wiki/Eclecticism_in_art "Eclecticism in art"), the progress of [medievalist](/wiki/Medievalism "Medievalism"), [orientalist](/wiki/Orientalism "Orientalism") and [folkloric](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore") studies, the growing participation of women in art production, the valorisation of [handicrafts](/wiki/Handicraft "Handicraft") and [applied arts](/wiki/Applied_arts "Applied arts"), opened up other fronts of appreciation for the visual arts, finding other truths worthy of appreciation that had previously been neglected and relegated to the margins of official culture.[[19]](#cite_note-Tanner,_Jeremy_2003-19)[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34) As a result of this great cultural transformation, the academic educational model, in order to survive, had to incorporate some of these innovations, but it broadly maintained the established tradition, and managed to become even more influential, continuing to inspire not only Europe, but also America and other countries colonized by Europeans, throughout the 19th century.[[35]](#cite_note-Kaufmann-35)

Another factor in this academic revival, even in the face of a profoundly changing scenario, was the reiteration of the idea of art as an instrument of political affirmation by nationalist movements in several countries.[[35]](#cite_note-Kaufmann-35)

### Apotheosis: Parisian salons and further influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Academic_art&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Apotheosis: Parisian salons and further influence")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Charles_X_distribuant_des_r%C3%A9compenses_aux_artistes_exposants_du_salon_de_1824_au_Louvre%2C_le_15_Janvier_1825_%28by_Fran%C3%A7ois_Joseph_Heim%29.jpg/330px-Charles_X_distribuant_des_r%C3%A9compenses_aux_artistes_exposants_du_salon_de_1824_au_Louvre%2C_le_15_Janvier_1825_%28by_Fran%C3%A7ois_Joseph_Heim%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_X_distribuant_des_r%C3%A9compenses_aux_artistes_exposants_du_salon_de_1824_au_Louvre,_le_15_Janvier_1825_(by_Fran%C3%A7ois_Joseph_Heim).jpg)

*[Charles X Distributing Awards to Artists](/wiki/Charles_X_Distributing_Awards_to_Artists "Charles X Distributing Awards to Artists")* for the [Paris Salon of 1824](/wiki/Paris_Salon_of_1824 "Paris Salon of 1824"). An 1827 painting by [François Joseph Heim](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Joseph_Heim "François Joseph Heim"), now in the [Louvre](/wiki/Louvre "Louvre").

The 19th century was the heyday of the academies, in the sense that their output became extremely well accepted among a much wider—but often less cultured and less demanding—public, giving academic art a popularity as great as that enjoyed today by [cinema](/wiki/Cinematography "Cinematography"), and with an equally popular theme, covering everything from traditional historical subjects to comic vignettes, from sweet and sentimental portraits to medievalist or picturesque scenes from exotic Eastern countries, something unthinkable during the Ancien Régime.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

By the second half of the 19th century, academic art had saturated European society. Exhibitions were held often, with the most popular exhibition being the [Paris Salon](/wiki/Paris_Salon "Paris Salon"), and beginning in 1903, the [Salon d'Automne](/wiki/Salon_d%27Automne "Salon d'Automne"). These [salons](/wiki/Salon_(gathering)#Public_salons "Salon (gathering)") were large scale events that attracted crowds of visitors, both native and foreign. As much a social affair as an artistic one, 50,000 people might visit on a single Sunday, and as many as 500,000 could see the exhibition during its two-month run. Thousands of paintings were displayed, hung from just below eye level all the way up to the ceiling in a manner now known as "Salon style". A successful showing at the salon was a seal of approval for an artist, making his work saleable to the growing ranks of private collectors. [Bouguereau](/wiki/William-Adolphe_Bouguereau "William-Adolphe Bouguereau"), [Alexandre Cabanel](/wiki/Alexandre_Cabanel "Alexandre Cabanel") and [Jean-Léon Gérôme](/wiki/Jean-L%C3%A9on_G%C3%A9r%C3%B4me "Jean-Léon Gérôme") were leading figures of this art world.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

During the reign of academic art, the paintings of the [Rococo](/wiki/Rococo "Rococo") era, previously held in low favor, were revived to popularity, and themes often used in Rococo art such as [Eros](/wiki/Eros_(god) "Eros (god)") and [Psyche](/wiki/Psyche_(mortal) "Psyche (mortal)") were popular again. The academic art world also admired Raphael, for the idealism of his work, in fact preferring him over Michelangelo.

#### England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Academic_art&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: England")]

In England, the influence of the Royal Academy grew as its association with the State consolidated. In the first half of the 19th century, the Royal Academy already exercised direct or indirect control over a vast network of galleries, museums, exhibitions and other artistic societies, and over a complex of administrative agencies that included the [Crown](/wiki/List_of_British_monarchs "List of British monarchs"), [parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") and other state departments, which found their cultural expression through their relations with the academic institution.[[39]](#cite_note-fyfe-39)[[40]](#cite_note-barlow-40) The [Royal Academy Summer Exhibition](/wiki/Royal_Academy_Summer_Exhibition "Royal Academy Summer Exhibition") gained momentum at the time and has been staged annually without interruption to the present day.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Frith_A_Private_View.jpg/330px-Frith_A_Private_View.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frith_A_Private_View.jpg)

*[A Private View at the Royal Academy, 1881](/wiki/A_Private_View_at_the_Royal_Academy,_1881 "A Private View at the Royal Academy, 1881")* by [William Powell Frith](/wiki/William_Powell_Frith "William Powell Frith"), depicting [Oscar Wilde](/wiki/Oscar_Wilde "Oscar Wilde") and other Victorian worthies at a [private view](/wiki/Private_view "Private view") of the [1881 exhibition](/wiki/Royal_Academy_Summer_Exhibition "Royal Academy Summer Exhibition")

As the century progressed, challenges to this primacy began to emerge, demanding that its relations with the government be clarified, and the institution began to pay more attention to market aspects in a society that was becoming more heterogeneous and cultivating multiple aesthetic tendencies. Subsidiary schools were also opened in various cities to meet regional demands. By the middle of the 19th century, the Royal Academy had already lost control over British artistic production, faced with the multiplication of independent creators and associations, but continued, facing internal tensions, to try to preserve it. Around 1860, it was again stabilized through new strategies of monopolizing power, incorporating new trends into its orbit, such as promoting the previously ignored technique of [watercolor](/wiki/Watercolor_painting "Watercolor painting"), which had become vastly popular, accepting the admission of women, requiring new members in an enlarged membership to renounce their affiliation to other societies and reforming its administrative structure to appear as a private institution, but imbued with a civic purpose and a public character. In this way, it managed to administer a significant part of the British artistic universe throughout the 19th century, and despite the opposition of societies and groups of artists such as the [Pre-Raphaelites](/wiki/Pre-Raphaelite_Brotherhood "Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood"), it managed to remain a disciplinary, educational and consecrating agency of the greatest importance, able to largely accompany the progress of [modernism](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism"), contradicting a common view that academies are invariably reactionary.[[39]](#cite_note-fyfe-39)[[40]](#cite_note-barlow-40)

#### Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Academic_art&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Germany")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Peter_von_Cornelius_001.jpg/330px-Peter_von_Cornelius_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Peter_von_Cornelius_001.jpg)

[Peter von Cornelius](/wiki/Peter_von_Cornelius "Peter von Cornelius"), of the [Nazarene](/wiki/Nazarene_movement "Nazarene movement") group: *The Parable of the Wise Virgins and the Foolish Virgins*, c. 1813, reverting to [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") patterns

In Germany, the academic spirit initially encountered some resistance to its full implementation. Already at the end of the 18th century, theorists such as [Baumgarten](/wiki/Alexander_Gottlieb_Baumgarten "Alexander Gottlieb Baumgarten"), [Schiller](/wiki/Schiller "Schiller") and [Kant](/wiki/Kant "Kant") had promoted the autonomy of Aesthetics through the concept of "[art for art's sake](/wiki/Art_for_art%27s_sake "Art for art's sake")", and had emphasized the importance of the artist's self-education, against the [massification](/wiki/Mass_culture "Mass culture") imposed by [civilization](/wiki/Civilization "Civilization") and its institutions, seeing the collectivizing structure and impersonal nature of academia as a threat to their desires for creative freedom, [individualistic](/wiki/Individualism "Individualism") inspiration, and absolute originality. In this vein, [art criticism](/wiki/Art_criticism "Art criticism") began to take on distinctly [sociological](/wiki/Sociology "Sociology") colors.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Part of this reaction was due to the activity of the [Nazarenes](/wiki/Nazarene_movement "Nazarene movement"), a group of painters who sought a return to a Renaissance style and medieval practices in a spirit of austerity and fraternity. Under their influence, masterclasses were introduced—paradoxically within the academies themselves—which sought to group promising students around a master, who was responsible for their instruction, but with much more concentrated attention and care than in the more generalist French system, based on the assumption that such more individualized treatment could provide a stronger and deeper education. This method was first instituted at the [Düsseldorf](/wiki/D%C3%BCsseldorf "Düsseldorf") Academy and progressed slowly, but over the course of the 19th century, it became common to all German academies, and was also imitated in other northern European countries. Interesting results of the masterclasses were the beginning of a tradition of large-scale mural painting and the steering of the local [avant-garde](/wiki/Avant-garde "Avant-garde") along less [iconoclastic](/wiki/Iconoclasm "Iconoclasm") lines than the Parisian ones.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

#### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Academic_art&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: United States")]

The influence of the Royal Academy extended across the ocean and strongly determined the foundation and direction of American art from the end of the 18th century until the middle of the 19th century, when the country began to establish its cultural independence. Some of the leading local artists studied in London under the guidance of the Royal Academy and others, who settled in England, continued to exert influence in their home country through regular submissions of works of art. This was the case with [John Singleton Copley](/wiki/John_Singleton_Copley "John Singleton Copley"), the dominant influence in his country until the beginning of the 19th century, and also with [Benjamin West](/wiki/Benjamin_West "Benjamin West"), who became one of the leaders of the English neoclassical-romantic movement and one of the main European names of his generation in the field of history painting. He made a number of fellow disciples, such as [Charles Willson Peale](/wiki/Charles_Willson_Peale "Charles Willson Peale"), [Gilbert Stuart](/wiki/Gilbert_Stuart "Gilbert Stuart") and [John Trumbull](/wiki/John_Trumbull "John Trumbull"), and his influence was similar to that of Copley on American painting.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Bierstadt_-_Among_the_Sierra_Nevada_Mountains_-_1868.jpg/330px-Bierstadt_-_Among_the_Sierra_Nevada_Mountains_-_1868.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bierstadt_-_Among_the_Sierra_Nevada_Mountains_-_1868.jpg)

[Albert Bierstadt](/wiki/Albert_Bierstadt "Albert Bierstadt"), *[Among the Sierra Nevada Mountains](/wiki/Among_the_Sierra_Nevada,_California "Among the Sierra Nevada, California")*, 1868. The landscape as a [patriotic](/wiki/Patriotism "Patriotism") celebration.

The first academy to be created in the United States was the [Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Academy_of_the_Fine_Arts "Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts"), founded in 1805 and still active today. The initiative came from the painter [Charles Peale](/wiki/Charles_Peale "Charles Peale") and the sculptor [William Rush](/wiki/William_Rush_(sculptor) "William Rush (sculptor)"), along with other artists and traders. Its progress was slow, and its peak was only reached at the end of the 19th century, when it began to receive significant financial support, opened a gallery and formed its own collection, becoming an anti-modernist bastion.[[44]](#cite_note-44) The most decisive step toward the formation of an American academic culture was taken when the [National Academy of Design](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Design "National Academy of Design") was founded in 1826 by [Samuel F. B. Morse](/wiki/Samuel_F._B._Morse "Samuel F. B. Morse"), [Asher B. Durand](/wiki/Asher_B._Durand "Asher B. Durand"), [Thomas Cole](/wiki/Thomas_Cole "Thomas Cole"), and other artists dissatisfied with the orientation of the Pennsylvania Academy. It soon became the most respected artistic institution in the country.[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46) Its method followed the traditional academic model, focusing on drawing from classical and live models, in addition to offering lectures on anatomy, perspective, history and mythology, among other subjects. Cole and Durand were also the founders of the [Hudson River School](/wiki/Hudson_River_School "Hudson River School"), an aesthetic movement that began a great painting tradition, lasting for three generations, with a remarkable unity of principles, and which presented the national landscape in an epic, idealistic and sometimes fanciful light. Its members included [Albert Bierstadt](/wiki/Albert_Bierstadt "Albert Bierstadt") and [Frederic Edwin Church](/wiki/Frederic_Edwin_Church "Frederic Edwin Church"), the most celebrated landscape painters of their generation.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/LincolnMemorialStatueNight.JPG/250px-LincolnMemorialStatueNight.JPG)](/wiki/File:LincolnMemorialStatueNight.JPG)

[Daniel Chester French](/wiki/Daniel_Chester_French "Daniel Chester French"), *[Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Statue_of_Abraham_Lincoln_(Lincoln_Memorial) "Statue of Abraham Lincoln (Lincoln Memorial)")*, 1920, in the [Lincoln Memorial](/wiki/Lincoln_Memorial "Lincoln Memorial"), [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")

In the field of sculpture, however, the greatest influence came from the Italian academies, especially through the example of [Antonio Canova](/wiki/Antonio_Canova "Antonio Canova"), who was the main figure of European neoclassicism, educated in part at the [Venice Academy](/wiki/Accademia_di_Belle_Arti_di_Venezia "Accademia di Belle Arti di Venezia") and in Rome.[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50) Italy offered a historical and cultural backdrop of irresistible interest to sculptors, with priceless monuments, ruins and collections, and working conditions were infinitely superior to those of the [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World"), where there was a shortage of both marble and capable assistants to help the artist in the complex and laborious art of stone carving and bronze casting. [Horatio Greenough](/wiki/Horatio_Greenough "Horatio Greenough") was just the first in a large wave of Americans to settle between Rome and Florence. The most notable of these was [William Wetmore Story](/wiki/William_Wetmore_Story "William Wetmore Story"), who after 1857 assumed leadership of the American colony that had been created in Rome, becoming a reference for all newcomers. Despite their stay in Italy, the group continued to be celebrated in their country, and their artistic achievements received continuous press coverage until the neoclassical vogue dissipated in North America from the 1870s onwards. By this time, the United States had already established its culture and created the general conditions to promote consistent and high-level local sculptural production, adopting an eclectic synthesis of styles.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53) These sculptors also strongly absorbed the influence of the French Académie, several of them were educated there, and their production populated most public spaces and the facades of major American buildings, with works of strong civic and great formalism that became icons of local culture, such as the [statue of Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Statue_of_Abraham_Lincoln_(Lincoln_Memorial) "Statue of Abraham Lincoln (Lincoln Memorial)") by [Daniel Chester French](/wiki/Daniel_Chester_French "Daniel Chester French") and the [memorial to Robert Gould Shaw](/wiki/Robert_Gould_Shaw_Memorial "Robert Gould Shaw Memorial") by [Augustus Saint-Gaudens](/wiki/Augustus_Saint-Gaudens "Augustus Saint-Gaudens").[[54]](#cite_note-Scott_&_Rutkoff-54)

In 1875, the [Art Students League](/wiki/Art_Students_League "Art Students League") took over as the leading American art academy, founded by students inspired by the model of the French Académie, establishing the guidelines for national art education until [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), while also opening its classes to women. Offering better working conditions than its Parisian model, the League was created by artists who saw in the French academic environment an appeal to culture and civilization and believed that this model would discipline the national [democratic](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") impulse, transcending regionalisms and social differences, refine the taste of [capitalists](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism") and contribute to elevating society and improving its culture.[[54]](#cite_note-Scott_&_Rutkoff-54)

#### Other countries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Academic_art&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Other countries")]

Academic art not only held influence in Western Europe and the United States, but also extended its influence to other countries. The artistic environment of Greece, for instance, was dominated by techniques from Western academies from the 17th century onward: this was first evident in the activities of the [Ionian school](/wiki/Ionian_school_(painting) "Ionian school (painting)"), and later became especially pronounced with the dawn of the [Munich school](/wiki/Greek_academic_art_of_the_19th_century "Greek academic art of the 19th century"). This was also true for [Latin American](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") nations, which, because their revolutions were modeled on the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), sought to emulate French culture. An example of a Latin American academic artist is [Ángel Zárraga](/wiki/%C3%81ngel_Z%C3%A1rraga "Ángel Zárraga") of [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"). Academic [art in Poland](/wiki/Art_in_Poland "Art in Poland") flourished under [Jan Matejko](/wiki/Jan_Matejko "Jan Matejko"), who established the [Kraków Academy of Fine Arts](/wiki/Jan_Matejko_Academy_of_Fine_Arts "Jan Matejko Academy of Fine Arts"). Many of these works can be seen in the [Gallery of 19th-Century Polish Art at Sukiennice](/wiki/Sukiennice_Museum "Sukiennice Museum") in [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków").

## Academic training

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Academic_art&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Academic training")]

### Teaching principles and cursus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Academic_art&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Teaching principles and cursus")]

The academies had as a basic assumption the idea that art could be taught through its systematization into a fully communicable body of theory and practice, minimizing the importance of creativity as an entirely original and individual contribution. Instead, they valued the emulation of established masters, venerating the classical tradition, and adopted collectively formulated concepts that had not only an aesthetic character, but also an ethical origin and purpose.

Young artists spent four years in rigorous training. In France, only students who passed an exam and carried a letter of reference from a noted professor of art were accepted at the academy's school, the [École des Beaux-Arts](/wiki/%C3%89cole_des_Beaux-Arts "École des Beaux-Arts") (School of Fine Arts). Drawings and paintings of the nude, called "académies", were the basic building blocks of academic art and the procedure for learning to make them was clearly defined. First, students copied prints after classical sculptures, becoming familiar with the principles of contour, light, and shade. The copy was believed crucial to the academic education; from copying works of past artists one would assimilate their methods of artmaking. To advance to the next step, and every successive one, students presented drawings for evaluation.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/%C3%89cole_des_beaux-arts_%28from_the_live%29.jpg/330px-%C3%89cole_des_beaux-arts_%28from_the_live%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:%C3%89cole_des_beaux-arts_(from_the_live).jpg)

Male art students painting "[from life](/wiki/Figure_drawing "Figure drawing")" at the [École des Beaux-Arts](/wiki/%C3%89cole_des_Beaux-Arts "École des Beaux-Arts") (School of Fine Arts) in Paris. Photographed late 1800s.

If approved, they would then draw from plaster casts of famous classical sculptures. Only after acquiring these skills were artists permitted entrance to classes in which a live model posed. Painting was not taught at the École des Beaux-Arts until after 1863. To learn to paint with a brush, the student first had to demonstrate proficiency in drawing, which was considered the foundation of academic painting. Only then could the pupil join the studio of an academician and learn how to paint. Throughout the entire process, competitions with a predetermined subject and a specific allotted period of time measured each student's progress.

The most famous art competition for students was the [Prix de Rome](/wiki/Prix_de_Rome "Prix de Rome"), whose winner was awarded a fellowship to study at the [Académie française](/wiki/Acad%C3%A9mie_fran%C3%A7aise "Académie française")'s [school at the Villa Medici](/wiki/French_Academy_in_Rome "French Academy in Rome") in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") for up to five years.[[55]](#cite_note-55) To compete, an artist had to be of French nationality, male, under 30 years of age, and single. He had to have met the entrance requirements of the École des Beaux-Arts and have the support of a well-known art teacher. The competition was grueling, involving several stages before the final one, in which 10 competitors were sequestered in studios for 72 days to paint their final history paintings. The winner was essentially assured a successful professional career.

As noted, a successful showing at the Salon, the exhibition of work founded by the École des Beaux-Arts, was a seal of approval for an artist. Artists petitioned the hanging committee for optimal placement "on the line", or at eye level. After the exhibition opened, artists complained if their works were "skyed", or hung too high. The ultimate achievement for the professional artist was election to membership in the Académie française and the right to be known as an academician. This depended on his consistency in exhibitions at the salons and the permanence of his production at a level of excellence.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

### Women artists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Academic_art&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Women artists")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Bashkirtseff_-_In_the_Studio.jpg/330px-Bashkirtseff_-_In_the_Studio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bashkirtseff_-_In_the_Studio.jpg)

[Robert-Fleury's](/wiki/Tony_Robert-Fleury "Tony Robert-Fleury") Atelier at the [Académie Julian](/wiki/Acad%C3%A9mie_Julian "Académie Julian") for female art students in Paris. An 1881 painting by student [Marie Bashkirtseff](/wiki/Marie_Bashkirtseff "Marie Bashkirtseff").

One effect of the move to academies was to make training more difficult for [women artists](/wiki/Women_artists "Women artists"), who were excluded from most academies until the last half of the 19th century.[[a]](#cite_note-fn1-58)[[58]](#cite_note-Myers-59)[[59]](#cite_note-60) This was partly because of concerns over the perceived impropriety presented by [nudity](/wiki/Nude_(art) "Nude (art)") during training.[[58]](#cite_note-Myers-59) In France, for example, the powerful École des Beaux-Arts had 450 members between the 17th century and the French Revolution, of which only 15 were women. Of those, most were daughters or wives of members. In the late 18th century, the French Academy resolved not to admit any women at all.[[b]](#cite_note-fn2-62) As a result, there are no extant large-scale history paintings by women from this period, though some women like [Marie-Denise Villers](/wiki/Marie-Denise_Villers "Marie-Denise Villers") and [Constance Mayer](/wiki/Constance_Mayer "Constance Mayer") made their name in other genres such as portraiture.[[61]](#cite_note-63)[[62]](#cite_note-64)[[63]](#cite_note-65)[[64]](#cite_note-66)

In spite of this, there were important steps forward for female artists. In Paris, the Salon became open to non-Academic painters in 1791, allowing women to showcase their work in the prestigious annual exhibition. Additionally, women were more frequently being accepted as students by famous artists such as [Jacques-Louis David](/wiki/Jacques-Louis_David "Jacques-Louis David") and [Jean-Baptiste Greuze](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Greuze "Jean-Baptiste Greuze").[[65]](#cite_note-Stranahan-67)

The emphasis in academic art on studies of the nude remained a considerable barrier for women studying art until the 20th century, both in terms of actual access to the classes and in terms of family and social attitudes to [middle-class](/wiki/Middle-class "Middle-class") women becoming artists.[[66]](#cite_note-Nochlin-68)

## Criticism and legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Academic_art&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Criticism and legacy")]

### Decline and the rise of modernism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Academic_art&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Decline and the rise of modernism")]

Academic art was first criticized for its use of [idealism](/wiki/Idealism_(art) "Idealism (art)"), by [Realist](/wiki/Realism_(arts) "Realism (arts)") artists such as [Gustave Courbet](/wiki/Gustave_Courbet "Gustave Courbet"), as being based on idealistic clichés and representing mythical and legendary motives while contemporary social concerns were being ignored. Another criticism by Realists was the "[false surface](/wiki/Licked_finish "Licked finish")" of paintings—the objects depicted looked smooth, slick, and idealized—showing no real texture. The Realist [Théodule Ribot](/wiki/Th%C3%A9odule_Ribot "Théodule Ribot") worked against this by experimenting with rough, unfinished textures in his painting.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/This_Year_Venuses_Again_%28Daumier%29_%28125970187%29.jpg/250px-This_Year_Venuses_Again_%28Daumier%29_%28125970187%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:This_Year_Venuses_Again_(Daumier)_(125970187).jpg)

[Caricature](/wiki/Caricature "Caricature") (French [bourgeoisie](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie")): *This Year Venuses Again… Always Venuses!*. [Honoré Daumier](/wiki/Honor%C3%A9_Daumier "Honoré Daumier"), No. 2 from series in *Le Charivati*, 1864.

Stylistically, the [Impressionists](/wiki/Impressionism "Impressionism"), who advocated quickly [painting outdoors](/wiki/Plein_air "Plein air") exactly what the eye sees and the hand puts down, criticized the finished and idealized painting style. Although academic painters began a painting by first making drawings and then painting [oil sketches](/wiki/Oil_paint "Oil paint") of their subject, the high polish they gave to their drawings seemed to the Impressionists tantamount to a lie, who disavowed the devotion to mechanical techniques.

Realists and Impressionists also defied the placement of still-life and landscape at the bottom of the hierarchy of genres. Most Realists and Impressionists and others among the early avant-garde who rebelled against academism were originally students in academic [ateliers](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/atelier "wikt:atelier"). [Claude Monet](/wiki/Claude_Monet "Claude Monet"), [Gustave Courbet](/wiki/Gustave_Courbet "Gustave Courbet"), [Édouard Manet](/wiki/%C3%89douard_Manet "Édouard Manet"), and even [Henri Matisse](/wiki/Henri_Matisse "Henri Matisse") were students under academic artists. Other artists, such as the [Symbolist painters](/wiki/Symbolism_(movement) "Symbolism (movement)") and some of the [Surrealists](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism"), were kinder to the tradition.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] As painters who sought to bring imaginary vistas to life, these artists were more willing to learn from a strongly representational tradition. Once the tradition had come to be looked on as old-fashioned, the [allegorical](/wiki/Allegory "Allegory") nudes and theatrically posed figures struck some viewers as bizarre and dreamlike.

As [modern art](/wiki/Modern_art "Modern art") and its avant-garde gained more power, academic art was further denigrated, and seen as sentimental, clichéd, conservative, non-innovative, [bourgeois](/wiki/Bourgeois "Bourgeois"), and "styleless". The French referred derisively to the style of academic art as *[L'art pompier](/wiki/L%27art_pompier "L'art pompier")* (*pompier* means "fireman") alluding to the paintings of [Jacques-Louis David](/wiki/Jacques-Louis_David "Jacques-Louis David") (who was held in esteem by the academy), which often depicted soldiers wearing fireman-like helmets.[[67]](#cite_note-69) It also suggests half-puns in French with *pompéien* ("from [Pompeii](/wiki/Pompeii "Pompeii")") and *pompeux* ("pompous").[[68]](#cite_note-70)[[69]](#cite_note-71) The paintings were called "grandes machines", which were said to have manufactured false emotion through contrivances and tricks.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Edouard_Manet_-_Luncheon_on_the_Grass_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/330px-Edouard_Manet_-_Luncheon_on_the_Grass_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edouard_Manet_-_Luncheon_on_the_Grass_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

[Édouard Manet](/wiki/%C3%89douard_Manet "Édouard Manet"), *[Le Déjeuner sur l'herbe](/wiki/Le_D%C3%A9jeuner_sur_l%27herbe "Le Déjeuner sur l'herbe")*, 1863, one of the rejected works exhibited at the [Salon des Refusés](/wiki/Salon_des_Refus%C3%A9s "Salon des Refusés"), now in the [Musée d'Orsay](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_d%27Orsay "Musée d'Orsay")

Faced with the dissatisfaction of a growing number of artists excluded from the official salons of the French Academy, in 1863 Emperor [Napoleon III](/wiki/Napoleon_III "Napoleon III") established the [Salon des Refusés](/wiki/Salon_des_Refus%C3%A9s "Salon des Refusés") (Salon of the Refused), which is considered one of the initial milestones of modernism.[[70]](#cite_note-Boime-72) Even with this concession, the public reaction was negative,[[71]](#cite_note-73) and an anonymous review published at the time summarizes the general attitude:

> This exhibition is sad and grotesque... save for one or two questionable exceptions, there is not a single work that deserves the honor of being shown in the official galleries. There is even something cruel about this exhibition, people laugh as if everything was nothing more than a farce.[[72]](#cite_note-74)

Following the example of Courbet, who in 1855 had opened a solo exhibition he called the Pavillon du Réalisme (Pavilion of Realism), in 1867 Manet, rejected from the official Salon, exhibited independently, and six years later a group of Impressionists founded the Salon des Indépendants (Salon of Independents). As a result of these initiatives, the art market began to open up to alternative schools, while dealers for new creators and private societies began aggressive campaigns to publicize their own artists, opening up various exhibition spaces to capture the interest of the bourgeois consumer public. Independent critics and literati also played an important role in shifting the economic and social center of gravity of the art system, protecting and promoting various non-academic artists and providing a kind of informal public education through the publication of articles in the press, which became a major forum for artistic debate, and one with a wide reach. In this process, the official institution of the academy, by then renamed the École des Beaux-Arts and having severed its connection with the government, began to lose ground rapidly, beginning its decline as a consecrating and educational institution.[[73]](#cite_note-75)[[74]](#cite_note-76)[[75]](#cite_note-77)

### Complete denigration and fall into obscurity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Academic_art&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Complete denigration and fall into obscurity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Clive_Bell.jpg/250px-Clive_Bell.jpg)](/wiki/File:Clive_Bell.jpg)

[Clive Bell](/wiki/Clive_Bell "Clive Bell"), of the [Bloomsbury Group](/wiki/Bloomsbury_Group "Bloomsbury Group") (pictured c. 1924), announced the "death" of art.

British art critic [Clive Bell](/wiki/Clive_Bell "Clive Bell"), linked to the [Bloomsbury Group](/wiki/Bloomsbury_Group "Bloomsbury Group") of English modernism, stated in 1914 that, by the middle of the 19th century, art had "died", losing all its aesthetic interest, and even tradition had ceased to exist.[[76]](#cite_note-78) This denigration of academic art reached its peak through the writings of American art critic [Clement Greenberg](/wiki/Clement_Greenberg "Clement Greenberg") who stated in 1939 that all academic art is "[kitsch](/wiki/Kitsch "Kitsch")", in the sense of banal, commercialist, and tried to associate academicism with the problems of industrial capitalism, in addition to linking a new concept of "good taste" with the ethics of left-wing, anti-bourgeois [political radicalism](/wiki/Radical_politics "Radical politics"). For him, the avant-garde was positive because it was an affective expression of a libertarian social conscience, and was therefore truer and freer, which was repeated ad infinitum afterwards, following the logic: academic = reactionary = bad, versus avant-garde = radical = good.[[77]](#cite_note-79)[[78]](#cite_note-80)

Several other influential critics, such as [Herbert Read](/wiki/Herbert_Read "Herbert Read") and [Ernst Gombrich](/wiki/Ernst_Gombrich "Ernst Gombrich"), devoted great efforts to breaking away from traditional academic standards. In all matters of teaching, not just in art teaching, great importance was given to creativity as the starting point for the learning process, preaching the abandonment of rules and formalisms, and aligning themselves with the proposals of educators and educational philosophers such as [Maria Montessori](/wiki/Maria_Montessori "Maria Montessori") and [Jean-Ovide Decroly](/wiki/Jean-Ovide_Decroly "Jean-Ovide Decroly"). Even several of the most important modern artists, such as [Kandinsky](/wiki/Kandinsky "Kandinsky"), [Klee](/wiki/Klee "Klee"), [Malevich](/wiki/Malevich "Malevich") and [Moholy-Nagy](/wiki/Moholy-Nagy "Moholy-Nagy"), dedicated themselves to creating schools and formulating new theories for art education based on these ideas, most notably the [Bauhaus](/wiki/Bauhaus "Bauhaus"), founded in [Weimar](/wiki/Weimar "Weimar"), Germany, in 1919 by [Walter Gropius](/wiki/Walter_Gropius "Walter Gropius"). For modernists, creativity was an innate faculty of perception and imagination, possessed by all people, and the less it was influenced by theories and norms, the richer and more fertile it would be. In this context, art education simply aimed to provide the means for this free creativity, guided by feelings and emotions, to be expressed materially as a work of art, a unique and original form that had its own syntax and did not depend on previous references.[[79]](#cite_note-81)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/6265_Dessau.JPG/330px-6265_Dessau.JPG)](/wiki/File:6265_Dessau.JPG)

Art schools such as the [Bauhaus](/wiki/Bauhaus "Bauhaus") (building in [Dessau](/wiki/Dessau "Dessau"), Germany, pictured) formulated theories for art education based on [modernist](/wiki/Modern_art "Modern art") ideas.

[Mannerist](/wiki/Mannerism "Mannerism"), Baroque, Rococo and neoclassical academic production managed to pass relatively unscathed by modernist criticism and secure its place in history, but eclectic academic trends of the second half of the 19th century were ridiculed and devalued to the point that, throughout the 20th century, most of these works were discarded from private collections, saw their market prices plummet and were removed from display in museums, relegated to oblivion in their storerooms.[[80]](#cite_note-Farmer-82)[[81]](#cite_note-Thompson-83)[[82]](#cite_note-84) By the 1950s, all the last practitioners of the old academicism had been cast into obscurity.
